channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Upgrades For MTG Silverquill Influence Commander Deck
item.content:encoded:
Ring! Class is back in session at Strixhaven with the Secrets of Strixhaven set, and today we’re attending Silverquill. In particular, we’ll be studying Silverquill Influence and looking at how we can upgrade the curriculum/deck for even more fun.

How does Silverquill Influence play?

Silverquill Influence is a control-ish goading enchantress style of deck that makes heavy use of auras to manipulate the board. It relies a fair amount on its commander Killian, Decisive Mentor to keep cards flowing and to force opponents into attacking each other until you suit up one of your creatures to win the 1v1 duel at the end that you have carefully arranged.

Silverquill Influence Commanders

While there may be an alternative commander in Scriv, the Obligator, make no question that this is Killian, Decisive Mentor's deck. He is really the second Orzhov enchantment focused commander, and he takes the deck down somewhat different paths than Eriette of the Charmed Apple, who is in the deck and really a better alternative commander choice for it.

Killian wants you to be dropping consistent enchantments to get goad on opposing creatures. Additionally, whenever enchanted creatures attack, you draw so you want to lean in on auras that you put on opposing creatures. Scriv is good at supporting this plan, but isn’t much for leading a deck on his own.

Upgrading Silverquill Influence

Eye of Nidhogg

From Final Fantasy Commander, the Eye of Nidhogg is a powerful goading enchantment similar to Coercive Impetus, and Ghoulish Impetus, both of which are already in the deck. This will pump a smaller creature, give it evasion, and force it to attack your opponents, drawing you cards with Killian, and as a cherry on top when it gets put into a graveyard, it comes back to your hand to do it again.

Sphere of Safety

While Ghostly Prison is already in Silverquill Influence, a bit more attack deterrence that isn’t goad would be good to include, and Sphere of Safety is very good at that. It scales the cost of attack to the number of enchantments you have, meaning your opponents will often be forced to pay 4, 5 or more mana to attack you with a single creature. You might also consider Summon: Yojimbo from Final Fantasy for another effect of this type, though it is limited by being a saga.

Shackles

Going back in time, I’m pulling up a card that I used to play casually when I started with Magic: The Gathering as Invasion was still around (I started with Onslaught block). Yes, I’m old, and no I’m not taking any questions about it.

None of that changes that Shackles is perfect for this deck as it provides a consistently returning enchantment that can also lockdown a creature if you need to do that. Because it doesn’t prevent attacks like a pacifism type, it can even go on the creature you want to goad, and then lock it down if need be. Alternatively, it can hop around on utility creatures that tap, while goading attackers, as it gives a consistent goad trigger, and card advantage with Killian.

Visions of Brutality

Sometimes, what you want is to make things really painful for your opponents. Visions of Brutality is an option to do just that as you goad an opponent’s creature and they take damage for all the damage it does. Putting this on an opposing green stompy monster like Ghalta or an Eldrazi is a way to make two of your opponents suffer massively all at once while you pull your little strings and make the game do what you want.

Nurgle's Rot

Cheap, efficient auras are at a premium in Silverquill Influence, and Nurgle’s Rot is as cheap as it gets at 1 mana. It doesn’t make the opposing creature deadlier, but it will give all the Killian, Decisive Mentor triggers, and if its designated target gets killed, you get a small chump blocker and the enchantment is back in your hand to do it again.

—
That’s all for this class about Silverquill Influence upgrades, but for continuing education about Secrets of Strixhaven commander decks, take a look at our course on the Lorehold Spirit upgrades.

A longtime lover of speculative fiction, in almost all its forms, Don joined TechRaptor in 2014 on a whim sending in an application as he was looking for… More about Don



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/upgrades-for-mtg-silverquill-influence-commander-deck
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Amazon Luna Drops Game Purchases, Stores, and Third-Party Subscriptions
item.content:encoded:
Amazon Luna will be losing certain elements of its service going forward, the company has announced, and that includes third-party subscriptions and game purchases.

As spotted by Wario64 on social media, Amazon Luna's service is changing starting from today; a subscription no longer offers "game stores, individual game purchases, or third-party subscriptions". This includes signups for Ubisoft+ and Jackbox's subscription service, as well as any games you've bought from storefronts like EA Play or GOG.

If you've bought games via Luna already, you'll still be able to play them up until June 10th, at which point the games will disappear. That's right; even if you've made the purchase, you won't be able to access the games you've picked up anymore.

It's not all doom and gloom, though. Depending on which platform you bought the games from, you can still access them on that platform, so if you purchased a game through EA Play, you'll just need to download the app and grab the game that way instead.

Amazon says that in place of the services that are departing, Luna is "doubling down on a broad range of gaming experiences", including third-party titles for streaming and the new Game Night feature.

It looks like some users are being offered Luna Premium subscriptions by way of "a thank you for being part of Amazon Luna", so if that's you, you'll get an email around June 10th with instructions on how to redeem your new membership.

All of this follows on from a major rebrand for Amazon Luna that saw the service pivot towards party games like the upcoming Masters of the Universe tie-in, as well as more casual fare rather than the core titles on which it previously focused (although many of those are still available with a membership).

You can sign up for Amazon Luna via the service's official website (but be aware it no longer offers third-party subscriptions or game purchases).

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/amazon-luna-games-stores-subscriptions
